html:
Hokkaidenko is not the “net cash plus hidden real estate” story in the older draft. It is better, and cleaner, as a small, ignored grid contractor whose earnings base is being pulled upward by Hokkaido’s power-demand reversal.

The facts changed. FY3/26 results were strong: revenue ¥72.45 billion, operating profit ¥4.97 billion, net income ¥3.72 billion, EPS ¥179.45, and ROE 10.6%. Orders were ¥76.32 billion and individual order backlog was ¥40.98 billion, up 9.9%, not the ¥100 billion-plus backlog suggested in the prior thesis. Hokkaidenko is a single equipment-construction segment, not a large real estate profit vehicle. The asset case is still useful, but the stock thesis should rest on current-cycle earnings and the next wave of grid work. FY2026 results

At ¥1,551, Hokkaidenko’s market cap is ¥32.16 billion. It has ¥12.66 billion of cash and deposits, ¥2.17 billion of investment securities, and no interest-bearing debt. The stock is 9.3x company-guided FY3/27 EPS of ¥166.04 and 0.87x book, while enterprise value is only about ¥17-20 billion against guided operating profit of ¥4.74 billion. That is roughly 3.7-4.1x forward EBIT for a regulated-infrastructure-adjacent contractor with a fortress balance sheet. Yahoo Finance Japan

The reason this is still interesting after the stock’s move from ¥1,053 in January to a recent high of ¥1,660 is that the market is still treating FY3/26 as a cyclical peak. The company’s own FY3/27 guidance calls for revenue growth to ¥79.2 billion, but operating profit down 4.7%. That embeds margin compression from 6.9% to about 6.0%. Maybe that happens for one year because of materials, labor, and mix. But management has just under-promised badly: FY3/26 actual net income beat the January revised forecast by more than 30%. The guide looks more like conservative Japanese budgeting than a real ceiling.

The variant perception is Hokkaido Electric’s demand curve. Hokkaido Electric’s 2026 plan says the old decline in regional electricity demand is reversing because of Rapidus, SoftBank’s large data center plans, renewable integration, and GX industrial siting. Its chart points from 276 hundred-million kWh toward roughly 340 and then 380 in the 2030s, a major change for a region investors still label as demographically shrinking. It is also adding grid interconnection capacity, studying further Hokkaido-Honshu links, expanding renewables, and dealing with 5.96 GW of installed renewable capacity versus a 5.07 GW winter peak load. That creates construction, substation, underground-line, grid-stability, battery, maintenance, and connection work. Hokkaidenko is the local field execution arm for exactly those jobs. Hokkaido Electric FY2026 plan

Customer concentration is usually a reason to avoid a contractor. Here, 76.6% of FY3/26 individual sales and 78.4% of orders came from Hokkaido Electric and Hokkaido Electric Network. That is the risk and the moat. The same parent/customer that suppresses governance upside also gives Hokkaidenko unusually visible demand. The 2026 plan even identifies Hokkaidenko’s role in renewable equipment design, construction, electrical maintenance, and productivity improvement, including a pole-setting process that lifted productivity roughly 3.5x.

The upside torque is not from idle cash. The dividend is only ¥20 per share, the payout ratio is 11-12%, and there is no meaningful buyback. Cash hoarding is a governance blemish. But even ignoring excess cash as a value unlock, the operating business is cheap. A company earning ¥3.4-3.7 billion after tax, with likely normalized free cash flow around ¥3.5-4.0 billion once working capital stabilizes, should not trade at ¥32 billion if the revenue base is moving from ¥72 billion toward ¥80-90 billion and the work mix is increasingly power, underground, data-center, semiconductor, and renewable interconnection.

Retail perception is noisy but useful. The Yahoo board is thinly posted, which matches the Sapporo-only liquidity problem, but the few comments focus on Rapidus, PBR 1x, “stingy” dividends, and whether the recent move is already over. That is not institutional sponsorship. It is an underfollowed stock being discovered by retail because the semiconductor/data-center angle finally showed up in reported results. Yahoo board

The bear case is straightforward. Labor scarcity and materials inflation could hold margins near 5.5-6.0% despite higher revenue. Hokkaido Electric controls the workstream and owns the majority, so minority shareholders may never get aggressive capital returns. Data centers and Rapidus could be delayed. The Sapporo listing makes liquidity poor. If FY3/26 was a one-off margin spike and FY3/27 guidance is accurate, the stock is merely cheap, not explosive.

The base case is that FY3/27 guidance is conservative, revenue reaches ¥82-86 billion over the next two years, operating margin settles around 6.5-7.0%, and EPS moves toward ¥200-220. Put only 10.5x earnings on that, still discounted for parent control and liquidity, and the stock is worth about ¥2,300.

Bear target: ¥1,150, down 26%. This assumes EPS falls to ¥150-160, margins reset lower, investors keep the stock at roughly 7x earnings and 0.65x book, and cash remains trapped.

Base target: ¥2,300, up 48%. This assumes revenue growth continues, margins normalize above guidance, EPS reaches ¥200-220, and the stock rerates to about 10.5x earnings or modestly above book.

Bull target: ¥3,600, up 132%. This assumes Hokkaido’s grid and GX buildout proves durable, revenue approaches ¥95-100 billion, operating profit reaches ¥7-8 billion, EPS reaches ¥260-300, and the market values the company at 12-13x earnings as a scarce listed Hokkaido electrification bottleneck.

Current stock price: ¥1,551 as of May 18, 2026.
